questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Sub-division
questions.stem: Wainganga Paper Ltd has 8,000 equity shares of ₹100 each, ₹60 paid up. It sub-divides them into shares of ₹10 each. Each new share is paid up to the extent of:

options.explanation: The paid/unpaid proportion must survive the split: 60/100 = 60%, so each new ₹10 share carries ₹6 paid up (80,000 shares in all).
₹4 is the UNPAID portion of each new share, not the paid-up portion.
₹60 was the paid-up amount on the old ₹100 share — the new share has a face value of only ₹10.
Fully paid new shares would quietly wipe out the shareholders' unpaid liability — sub-division must preserve the 60% paid proportion.

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · s.61 procedure
questions.stem: To alter its share capital under section 61 (consolidation, sub-division, conversion to stock, cancellation of unissued shares), a company needs:

options.text: Authorisation in its articles and an ordinary resolution in general meeting
options.explanation: s.61 alterations are the light-touch route: articles must permit, and an ordinary resolution suffices — no tribunal.

options.text: A board resolution alone
options.explanation: The board cannot alter the capital clause by itself — members must pass an ordinary resolution.

options.text: Approval of creditors holding two-thirds in value
options.explanation: Creditor consent belongs to reduction and schemes — an alteration touches nobody's paid-up money, so creditors have no say.

options.text: A special resolution confirmed by the Tribunal
options.explanation: Special resolution + NCLT confirmation is the s.66 REDUCTION procedure — alteration is deliberately easier.

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Consolidation
questions.stem: Indravati Chemicals Ltd consolidates its 5,00,000 fully paid equity shares of ₹2 each into shares of ₹10 each. The number of shares after consolidation is:

options.text: 1,00,000 shares
options.explanation: Capital stays ₹10,00,000; five ₹2 shares roll into one ₹10 share → 5,00,000 ÷ 5 = 1,00,000 shares.

options.text: 5,00,000 shares
options.explanation: An unchanged count with a higher face value would multiply capital five times — consolidation repackages, never creates.

options.text: 50,000 shares
options.explanation: This divides by 10 — the ratio is new denomination ÷ old = 5, not 10.

options.text: 2,50,000 shares
options.explanation: This halves the count — the denomination rose fivefold (₹2 → ₹10), so the count falls fivefold.

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Cancelling unissued shares
questions.stem: A company cancels 1,00,000 authorised shares that were never taken or agreed to be taken by anyone. This is:

options.text: A reduction of capital requiring Tribunal confirmation
options.explanation: s.66 governs cancelling PAID-UP capital — unissued shares have no paid-up money in them.

options.text: Prohibited — authorised capital can only be increased
options.explanation: s.61 expressly permits cancelling shares not taken or agreed to be taken.

options.text: An alteration under s.61 — it trims authorised capital and is NOT a reduction of share capital
options.explanation: s.61(1)(e) cancellation touches only the unissued, authorised figure; no paid-up money is affected, so the s.66 machinery (special resolution, Tribunal) is not attracted.

options.text: A buyback of securities
options.explanation: A buyback purchases ISSUED shares from holders for cash — these shares were never issued.

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Reduction of paid-up value
questions.stem: Under a sanctioned scheme, Panchganga Motors Ltd reduces its 60,000 equity shares of ₹10 fully paid to ₹6 paid up per share, the face value remaining ₹10. The credit to the Capital Reduction Account is:

options.explanation: ₹3,60,000 is the capital that REMAINS (60,000 × 6) — the credit is the amount given up.
The sacrifice is ₹4 per share: 60,000 × 4 = ₹2,40,000 credited to Capital Reduction.

options.text: Nil
options.explanation: Retaining the face value does not negate the reduction — paid-up value fell by ₹4 per share, and that sacrifice is the credit.
That is the entire original capital — only the cancelled slice moves to Capital Reduction.

questions.sourceRef: Companies Act 2013, s.66(1)(b)(ii); SM Ch 14 entries

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · s.66 procedure
questions.stem: A reduction of share capital under section 66 requires:

options.text: A special resolution alone, with no external confirmation
options.explanation: The special resolution is necessary but not sufficient — the Tribunal must confirm before the reduction takes effect.

options.text: Only a board resolution and a registered valuer's certificate
options.explanation: Neither substitutes for the members' special resolution and the Tribunal's confirmation.

options.text: A special resolution and confirmation by the Tribunal
options.explanation: Because reduction shrinks the creditors' cushion, s.66 demands a special resolution plus NCLT confirmation on the company's application.

options.text: An ordinary resolution, if the articles authorise
options.explanation: That is the s.61 ALTERATION procedure — reduction is the heavier surgery with heavier safeguards.

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Both values reduced
questions.stem: Koyna Turbines Ltd reduces its 25,000 equity shares of ₹100 fully paid to ₹25 fully paid (face value also reduced to ₹25). The credit to the Capital Reduction Account is:

options.explanation: ₹6,25,000 is the NEW capital (25,000 × 25) — the credit is what was given up, not what remains.
A 50% write-down would give this — the scheme cut the shares by 75%, from ₹100 to ₹25.
Sacrifice = ₹75 per share × 25,000 = ₹18,75,000; the old ₹100 capital account closes, the new ₹25 account opens, and the difference feeds Capital Reduction.
The whole old capital cannot be the sacrifice — ₹6,25,000 of it survives as the new paid-up capital.

questions.sourceRef: Companies Act 2013, s.66(1)(b)(ii); SM Ch 14 entries

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Extinguishing uncalled liability
questions.stem: Sina Foundries Ltd has 50,000 equity shares of ₹10 each, ₹8 paid up. The scheme cancels the uncalled ₹2 per share, making the shares ₹8 fully paid. The amount credited to the Capital Reduction Account is:

options.explanation: That is the paid-up capital that CONTINUES (50,000 × 8) — untouched by this mode of reduction.
50,000 × ₹2 is the uncalled amount extinguished — but uncalled money was never paid in, so it cannot fund any write-off.
The full face value never moves — only the label on the shares changes, from ₹10 partly paid to ₹8 fully paid.

options.text: Nil
options.explanation: Cancelling UNCALLED capital extinguishes a future liability — no paid-up money is sacrificed, so nothing is freed to write losses against. The entry merely swaps partly paid ₹10 shares for fully paid ₹8 shares.

questions.sourceRef: Companies Act 2013, s.66(1)(a); SM Ch 14 entries

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Creditors' sacrifice
questions.stem: Under a scheme of arrangement, trade creditors of Bhadra Ceramics Ltd amounting to ₹4,80,000 agree to accept 75 paise in the rupee in full settlement. The credit to the Reconstruction Account is:

options.explanation: ₹3,60,000 (75%) is what the creditors will still be PAID — the credit is only the slice they gave up.
The full claim is not sacrificed — three-quarters of it survives as a payable.
The sacrifice is the 25% forgone: 4,80,000 × 25% = ₹1,20,000 — Creditors Dr 1,20,000 → To Reconstruction.
This applies a 20% sacrifice — accepting 75 paise per rupee means forgoing 25 paise, not 20.

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 14 — settlement of outside liabilities

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Debenture holders' sacrifice
questions.stem: Holders of 12% debentures of ₹5,00,000 in Netravati Infra Ltd agree to exchange them for new 10% debentures equal to 85% of their claim. The sacrifice credited to the Reconstruction Account is:

options.explanation: The old debentures are not written off entirely — 85% of the claim survives in new form.
That is the NEW debenture liability issued — the reconstruction credit is only the portion surrendered.
This takes a 10% sacrifice — the exchange is at 85%, so 15% is forgone. (The coupon drop from 12% to 10% affects future interest, not this principal computation.)

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 14 — settlement of outside liabilities

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Asset appreciation
questions.stem: As part of a reconstruction scheme, land of Manjira Estates Ltd with a book value of ₹3,00,000 is revalued at ₹4,20,000. The entry is:

options.text: Land A/c Dr ₹4,20,000 → To Capital Reduction A/c ₹4,20,000
options.explanation: Only the INCREASE of ₹1,20,000 is recorded — the ₹3,00,000 book value already sits in the land account.

options.text: Land A/c Dr ₹1,20,000 → To Capital Reduction A/c ₹1,20,000
options.explanation: An appreciation found during the scheme is a credit the scheme can use: debit the asset up by ₹1,20,000, credit Capital Reduction.

options.text: No entry — appreciation cannot be recognised in a reconstruction
options.explanation: The SM treatment expressly credits appreciations to Capital Reduction — schemes routinely revalue both ways.

options.text: Land A/c Dr ₹1,20,000 → To Revaluation Reserve A/c ₹1,20,000
options.explanation: In a reconstruction the surplus is marshalled into the Capital Reduction Account to absorb write-offs — parking it in a revaluation reserve would defeat the scheme.

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Write-off total
questions.stem: A scheme requires Kundalika Motors Ltd to write off: P&L debit balance ₹2,60,000, goodwill ₹80,000, preliminary expenses ₹15,000 and overvalued patents ₹45,000. The total debit to the Capital Reduction Account is:

options.explanation: This omits preliminary expenses — fictitious assets are always in the write-off list.
2,60,000 + 80,000 + 15,000 + 45,000 = ₹4,00,000 — every hollow asset and the accumulated loss go out through Capital Reduction.
This omits goodwill — a worthless intangible is precisely what the scheme exists to remove.
This omits the ₹45,000 patents write-down — overvalued assets are written off through the account like the rest.

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Balance of the account
questions.stem: After all scheme entries, the Capital Reduction Account of Penganga Tools Ltd shows credits of ₹5,10,000 against write-offs of ₹4,72,000. The remaining balance is:

options.text: ₹38,000, credited to the Statement of P&L
options.explanation: Routing a capital sacrifice through profit would let the scheme manufacture earnings — it goes to capital reserve.

options.text: ₹38,000, transferred to General Reserve
options.explanation: The destination is CAPITAL reserve — the surplus arose from capital sacrifices, not trading, and stays non-distributable.

options.text: Nil — the account must always close out exactly
options.explanation: Schemes rarely land exactly; the account closes BY the transfer of its ₹38,000 balance to capital reserve.

options.text: ₹38,000, transferred to Capital Reserve
options.explanation: A credit balance left after the write-offs goes to Capital Reserve under Reserves and Surplus — 5,10,000 − 4,72,000 = ₹38,000.

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 14 — balance of reconstruction account

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Shortfall in the account
questions.stem: If the write-offs a scheme demands EXCEED the total credits in the Reconstruction Account, the gap is bridged by:

options.text: Drawing on the existing reserves in the books
options.explanation: The SM treatment: where sacrifices fall short, reserves already standing in the books are applied against the remaining write-offs.

options.text: Debiting the Tribunal-approved amount to share premium without limit
options.explanation: Securities premium can participate only as the scheme and law provide — the standing answer for a shortfall is the company's existing reserves.

options.text: Carrying the excess forward as goodwill
options.explanation: Manufacturing an asset out of unabsorbed losses would repeat the disease the scheme is curing.

options.text: Leaving the assets overstated to the extent of the gap
options.explanation: A scheme that leaves the books dishonest has failed its one job — the write-offs must be completed.

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 14 — balance of reconstruction account

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · "and Reduced"
questions.stem: The words "and Reduced" are added after the company's name following a capital reduction:

options.text: Only if the Tribunal so orders
options.explanation: The suffix is not automatic — it appears only when the confirming Tribunal directs it as a warning flag to those dealing with the company.

options.text: Whenever the reduction exceeds 50% of paid-up capital
options.explanation: No percentage trigger exists — the Tribunal's discretion is the only test.

options.text: In every case, for five years
options.explanation: Five years is the disclosure period for amounts written off fixed assets — the name suffix itself depends entirely on the Tribunal's order.

options.text: Never — the practice was abolished by the Companies Act 2013
options.explanation: The practice survives; it simply operates at the Tribunal's direction.

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Five-year disclosure
questions.stem: The amount written off fixed assets under a reconstruction scheme must be shown in the company's accounts for:

options.text: Ten years
options.explanation: Ten years has no basis here — the disclosure period is five.

options.text: Five years
options.explanation: The SM's presentation rule: the write-off remains visible in the fixed-asset disclosures for five years, keeping the surgery on record.

options.text: Until the asset is disposed of
options.explanation: The rule is time-boxed at five years, not tied to the asset's remaining life.

options.text: One year
options.explanation: A single year would let the write-down vanish from view immediately — the prescribed window is five years.

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Surrender — cancellation
questions.stem: In a surrender scheme, shareholders of Ghataprabha Mills Ltd surrender shares with a paid-up value of ₹7,20,000. Shares worth ₹5,20,000 are re-allotted to creditors in settlement of their claims. The surrendered shares remaining are cancelled. The credit to the Reconstruction Account on cancellation is:

options.text: Nil
options.explanation: Surrendered shares that find no taker do not quietly return to shareholders — they are cancelled through the Reconstruction Account.
Unutilised surrendered capital = 7,20,000 − 5,20,000 = ₹2,00,000, cancelled and credited to the Reconstruction Account.
That portion was RE-ALLOTTED, not cancelled — it lives on as capital in the creditors' hands.
₹5,20,000 of the surrendered shares went back out to creditors — only the unutilised remainder is cancelled.

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Surrender — purpose
questions.stem: In the surrender-of-shares method of internal reconstruction, the surrendered shares are primarily used to:

options.text: Re-allot to debenture holders and creditors so their claims against the company reduce
options.explanation: That is the engine of the method: sub-divide, surrender, and hand the surrendered shares to outside claimants in discharge of their dues; the leftovers are cancelled.

options.text: Sell in the market to raise fresh working capital
options.explanation: A distressed company hawking its own surrendered shares is not the scheme — they go to existing claimants, not the market.

options.text: Hold as treasury stock for future employee options
options.explanation: Indian company law does not permit treasury shares — surrendered shares are either re-allotted under the scheme or cancelled.

options.text: Distribute pro-rata to the remaining shareholders as bonus
options.explanation: Handing them back to shareholders would undo the surrender — the point is to move value to creditors.

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Scheme expenses
questions.stem: Expenses incurred in carrying out a scheme of internal reconstruction are:

options.text: Charged to the Statement of P&L of the following year
options.explanation: The costs belong to the scheme, not to next year's operations — they go through the reconstruction account now.

options.text: Debited to the Capital Reduction (Reconstruction) Account
options.explanation: Scheme costs are part of the reconstruction itself and are absorbed by the account along with the write-offs.

options.text: Capitalised as deferred revenue expenditure
options.explanation: Creating a new fictitious asset in the very scheme meant to remove them would be self-defeating.

options.text: Adjusted against securities premium in all cases
options.explanation: Securities premium participates only where the scheme provides — the default home for scheme expenses is the reconstruction account.

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · New paid-up capital
questions.stem: Varada Cements Ltd has 30,000 equity shares of ₹100 fully paid. A sanctioned scheme reduces them to ₹40 fully paid. The paid-up share capital after the scheme is:

options.explanation: This confuses the ₹10 face-value convention with the stated ₹40 — the new paid-up value per share is ₹40.
30,000 × ₹40 = ₹12,00,000 — the balance-sheet capital line after the scheme.
₹18,00,000 (30,000 × 60) is the amount CANCELLED and credited to Capital Reduction — not what survives.
That is the pre-scheme capital — the reduction cut each share from ₹100 to ₹40.

questions.sourceRef: Companies Act 2013, s.66; SM Ch 14 entries

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Internal vs external
questions.stem: The essential difference between internal and external reconstruction is that in internal reconstruction:

options.text: A new company takes over the business of the old one
options.explanation: That is EXTERNAL reconstruction — the defining feature of the internal variety is that no new entity appears.

options.text: The accounting follows AS 14
options.explanation: AS 14 governs amalgamations including external reconstruction — internal reconstruction runs on the Capital Reduction Account, not AS 14.

options.text: Creditors can never be asked to sacrifice
options.explanation: Compromises with creditors and debenture holders are a standard limb of internal schemes.

options.text: The same company survives and absorbs its own losses; no new company is formed
options.explanation: Internal reconstruction reorganises capital within the existing entity. External reconstruction forms a NEW company to take over the business (accounted under AS 14) and winds the old one up.

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · When the account opens
questions.stem: Which of these methods of internal reconstruction does NOT involve the Capital Reduction / Reconstruction Account?

options.text: Reduction of share capital by cancelling lost capital
options.explanation: This is the account's core use — the cancelled capital is its opening credit.

options.text: A compromise in which creditors accept part payment
options.explanation: The creditors' sacrifice is credited to the reconstruction account.

options.text: Sub-division of shares and variation of shareholders' rights
options.explanation: Alterations of capital (s.61) and rights variations (s.48) rearrange packaging — nobody sacrifices paid-up money, so no reconstruction account opens. It is needed for reduction, compromises and surrender.

options.text: Surrender of shares with cancellation of the unutilised portion
options.explanation: The cancellation credit flows through the reconstruction account.

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 14 — entries on reconstruction (which methods open the account)

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Reserves given up
questions.stem: Under a scheme, the equity shareholders of Hemavati Agro Ltd give up their claim over the general reserve of ₹2,50,000 so it can absorb accumulated losses. The entry is:

options.text: Reconstruction A/c Dr ₹2,50,000 → To General Reserve A/c ₹2,50,000
options.explanation: This runs backwards — it would CREATE a reserve out of the scheme instead of absorbing one into it.

options.text: General Reserve A/c Dr ₹2,50,000 → To Capital Reserve A/c ₹2,50,000
options.explanation: Capital reserve receives only the FINAL surplus of the reconstruction account — reserves given up route through the account first.

options.text: General Reserve A/c Dr ₹2,50,000 → To Reconstruction A/c ₹2,50,000
options.explanation: Reserves surrendered by shareholders are marshalled into the Reconstruction Account, joining the pool that absorbs the write-offs.

options.text: No entry — reserves cannot be applied in a reconstruction
options.explanation: Reserves given up by shareholders are a standard credit in schemes — the SM lists this entry expressly.

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 14 — shareholders giving up reserves

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Modes of reduction
questions.stem: Section 66 permits a company to reduce its share capital in which of the following ways?

options.text: Only by cancelling capital that is lost
options.explanation: Cancelling lost capital is one mode of three — extinguishing uncalled liability and paying off excess capital are equally valid.

options.text: Extinguish liability for unpaid capital; pay off excess capital; cancel lost capital
options.explanation: The three statutory modes: relieve shareholders of uncalled amounts, return capital surplus to requirements, and cancel paid-up capital unrepresented by assets — the third being the reconstruction workhorse.

options.text: Only by paying off capital in cash
options.explanation: Pay-off is one mode; the other two involve no cash at all.

options.text: By cancelling unissued authorised shares
options.explanation: That is a s.61 ALTERATION — unissued shares hold no paid-up money, so cancelling them is not a reduction.

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Paying off excess capital
questions.stem: Amaravati Textiles Ltd, flush with cash, returns ₹25 per share on its 10,000 preference shares of ₹100 fully paid (reducing them to ₹75 paid). Paid-up capital falls by:

options.text: ₹2,50,000 — and nothing is credited to Capital Reduction
options.explanation: 10,000 × 25 = ₹2,50,000 leaves as cash to shareholders. Value returned, not sacrificed — so this mode generates no reconstruction credit.
Only the ₹25 slice per share is paid off — ₹75 per share stays as capital.
₹7,50,000 is the capital REMAINING (10,000 × 75), not the reduction.

options.text: ₹2,50,000, credited to the Capital Reduction Account
options.explanation: The amount is right but the destination is wrong — cash went back to shareholders, so there is no sacrifice to credit to the account.

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Confirming authority
questions.stem: The authority that confirms a reduction of share capital under section 66 is:

options.text: The National Company Law Tribunal
options.explanation: The company applies to the Tribunal after the special resolution; the NCLT hears creditors' representations before confirming.

options.text: The Registrar of Companies
options.explanation: The RoC registers the confirmed reduction afterwards — confirmation itself is the Tribunal's.

options.text: The Regional Director
options.explanation: The Regional Director has other compromises to bless — s.66 confirmation belongs to the Tribunal.

options.text: SEBI, for all companies
options.explanation: SEBI's writ runs over listed-company processes, not the s.66 confirmation, which is the Tribunal's for every company.

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Full account
questions.stem: Aganashini Marine Ltd's scheme produces these Reconstruction Account entries — credits: capital reduction ₹6,00,000, creditors' sacrifice ₹90,000, appreciation of land ₹60,000; debits: P&L debit balance ₹4,80,000, goodwill ₹1,20,000, machinery written down ₹90,000, scheme expenses ₹20,000. The transfer to Capital Reserve is:

options.explanation: Credits 7,50,000 − debits 7,10,000 = ₹40,000 to Capital Reserve.

options.text: Nil
options.explanation: The account does not net to zero here — credits exceed debits by ₹40,000, and that surplus must go somewhere: capital reserve.
That is the debit side total — the capital reserve gets only the excess of credits over debits.
That is the gross credit side — the write-offs must be set against it first.

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 14 — balance of reconstruction account

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Conversion to stock
questions.stem: Under section 61, a company may convert into stock:

options.text: Shares held for more than one year
options.explanation: Holding period is irrelevant — the only statutory condition is that the shares be fully paid.

options.text: Only preference shares
options.explanation: No class restriction of this kind exists — the test is full payment, not class.

options.text: Any shares, whether fully or partly paid
options.explanation: Partly paid shares cannot become stock — the unpaid-call machinery needs identifiable shares.

options.text: Only its fully paid-up shares
options.explanation: Stock is the fused, transferable-in-any-amount form of capital — only FULLY PAID shares can be converted (and stock can be reconverted into fully paid shares).

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Case: Vetravati Engineering (full scheme)
questions.case: Vetravati Engineering Ltd has been unprofitable for six years. Its balance sheet shows: 80,000 equity shares of ₹10 fully paid (₹8,00,000); 3,000 9% preference shares of ₹100 fully paid (₹3,00,000); trade creditors ₹2,40,000; P&L debit balance ₹5,80,000; goodwill ₹1,00,000; obsolete inventory overvalued by ₹60,000; preliminary expenses ₹10,000. The Tribunal-confirmed scheme provides: equity shares reduced to ₹2.50 fully paid (face value ₹2.50); preference shares reduced to ₹75 fully paid (face value ₹75); creditors forgo 25% of their claims; freehold property is appreciated by ₹90,000; all fictitious and lost assets are written off; any balance goes to capital reserve.

questions.stem: The credit to Capital Reduction from the equity shareholders is:

options.explanation: The whole old capital is not sacrificed — ₹2,00,000 survives as the new paid-up capital.
₹2,00,000 (80,000 × 2.50) is what REMAINS, not what was given up.
This multiplies ₹7.50 by 1,00,000 shares — the company has 80,000 shares.
Sacrifice = ₹7.50 per share × 80,000 = ₹6,00,000; the shares continue as ₹2.50 fully paid.

questions.sourceRef: Companies Act 2013, s.66(1)(b)(ii); SM Ch 14 entries

questions.stem: Total credits to the Capital Reduction Account under the scheme are:

options.explanation: This omits the creditors' ₹60,000 sacrifice — a compromise credit belongs in the account.
This omits the preference shareholders' ₹75,000 sacrifice (₹100 → ₹75 per share on 3,000 shares).
This omits the ₹90,000 appreciation of freehold property, which the scheme credits to the account.
Equity 6,00,000 + preference (3,000 × ₹25) 75,000 + creditors (25% × 2,40,000) 60,000 + property appreciation 90,000 = ₹8,25,000.

questions.stem: Total write-offs debited to the Capital Reduction Account are:

options.explanation: P&L 5,80,000 + goodwill 1,00,000 + inventory overvaluation 60,000 + preliminary expenses 10,000 = ₹7,50,000.
This omits the ₹10,000 preliminary expenses — fictitious assets are always in the write-off list.
This omits goodwill — a worthless intangible is the first thing a scheme removes.
This omits the ₹60,000 inventory overvaluation — overvalued current assets are written down through the account too.

questions.stem: The balance transferred to Capital Reserve is:

options.explanation: Credits 8,25,000 − write-offs 7,50,000 = ₹75,000 to Capital Reserve.
That is the gross credit side; ₹7,50,000 of it was consumed by the write-offs.
This double-counts — the surplus is a single ₹75,000.

options.text: Nil
options.explanation: The scheme's credits exceed its debits by ₹75,000 — that surplus must be parked in capital reserve.

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 14 — balance of reconstruction account

questions.stem: Vetravati must add "and Reduced" to its name:

options.text: Never — the suffix applies only to voluntary winding up
options.explanation: The practice belongs to capital reduction, applied when the Tribunal orders it.

options.text: Automatically, because the reduction exceeded 50%
options.explanation: There is no percentage trigger — only the Tribunal's direction matters.

options.text: Automatically, for five years
options.explanation: Five years is the fixed-asset write-off disclosure window, not a name rule.

options.text: Only if the Tribunal's confirmation order so directs
options.explanation: The suffix operates at the Tribunal's discretion — it is a warning label the NCLT may or may not require.

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Case: Tapti Sugars (alteration)
questions.case: Tapti Sugars Ltd, authorised by its articles, resolves in general meeting to sub-divide its 20,000 equity shares of ₹50 each, ₹40 paid up, into equity shares of ₹10 each. No other change is made to the capital structure.

questions.stem: After the sub-division, the number of equity shares is:

options.text: 2,00,000 shares
options.explanation: A tenfold count needs a ₹5 new denomination — the resolution says ₹10.

options.text: 1,00,000 shares
options.explanation: Each ₹50 share splits into five ₹10 shares: 20,000 × 5 = 1,00,000.

options.text: 20,000 shares
options.explanation: An unchanged count with a smaller face value would shrink capital — sub-division multiplies the count instead.

options.text: 40,000 shares
options.explanation: A doubling would follow a ₹50 → ₹25 split; the split here is ₹50 → ₹10, i.e. fivefold.

questions.stem: The paid-up amount on each new ₹10 share is:

options.explanation: The paid proportion (40/50 = 80%) must be preserved: 80% × ₹10 = ₹8 paid up, ₹2 uncalled.
₹2 is the UNPAID amount per new share.
Making the new shares fully paid would extinguish the shareholders' unpaid liability — that needs a s.66 reduction, not a sub-division.
₹40 was the paid-up amount on the old ₹50 share — each new share is only a fifth of it.

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 14 — proportion rule on sub-division

questions.stem: The Capital Reduction Account required for this sub-division is:

options.text: None — an alteration under s.61 never opens the account
options.explanation: Paid-up capital is unchanged (1,00,000 × ₹8 = 20,000 × ₹40); nothing is sacrificed, so there is nothing to credit.

options.text: Credited with ₹2,00,000
options.explanation: The ₹2,00,000 uncalled amount neither disappears nor moves — it remains callable on the new shares.

options.text: Credited with ₹8,00,000
options.explanation: ₹8,00,000 is simply the continuing paid-up capital — no part of it was given up.

options.text: Debited with the sub-division expenses only
options.explanation: Routine alteration costs are revenue expenses; the reconstruction account does not open for an alteration at all.

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 14 — which methods open the account

questions.stem: The resolution and approvals Tapti needed were:

options.text: Consent of 75% of creditors in value
options.explanation: Creditors are unaffected by a repackaging of shares — their consent is not required.

options.text: An ordinary resolution, with authority in the articles — no Tribunal involvement
options.explanation: s.61 alterations run on articles + ordinary resolution; the NCLT enters only for s.66 reductions.

options.text: A board resolution ratified by the Registrar
options.explanation: Members must resolve; the Registrar only receives the notice of alteration afterwards.

options.text: A special resolution confirmed by the NCLT
options.explanation: That is the s.66 reduction procedure — a sub-division is an alteration, not a reduction.

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Case: Pennar Fabrics (surrender)
questions.case: Pennar Fabrics Ltd has 30,000 equity shares of ₹20 fully paid. Its sanctioned scheme: (1) sub-divide each share into five shares of ₹4 each; (2) shareholders surrender 50% of their holdings to the company; (3) creditors owed ₹2,60,000 accept 60,000 of the surrendered shares (₹4 each, ₹2,40,000) in full settlement of their claims; (4) surrendered shares not re-allotted are cancelled.

questions.stem: The paid-up value of the shares surrendered is:

options.explanation: That is the entire capital — only half the holdings are surrendered.
This halves the value twice — 50% of the ₹6,00,000 capital is ₹3,00,000.
75,000 is the NUMBER of shares surrendered — their paid-up value is that count × ₹4.
After sub-division: 1,50,000 shares of ₹4. Surrender 50% → 75,000 shares × ₹4 = ₹3,00,000.

questions.stem: The sacrifice by creditors credited to the Reconstruction Account is:

options.explanation: The whole claim was not forgone — shares worth ₹2,40,000 discharged most of it.
₹2,40,000 is the value of shares the creditors RECEIVED — their sacrifice is the shortfall against the claim.
Claims of ₹2,60,000 settled with shares worth ₹2,40,000 → sacrifice ₹20,000.
₹60,000 confuses the cancelled-shares figure with the creditors' sacrifice.

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 14 — surrender of shares; settlement of liabilities

questions.stem: The credit to the Reconstruction Account on cancelling the unutilised surrendered shares is:

options.explanation: Surrendered 75,000 shares − re-allotted 60,000 = 15,000 shares × ₹4 = ₹60,000 cancelled to the Reconstruction Account.
That is the full surrendered value — 60,000 of those shares went to creditors and live on.
15,000 is the count of cancelled shares; the rupee credit is that count × ₹4.
The re-allotted shares are NOT cancelled — they became the creditors' capital.

questions.stem: The total credited to the Reconstruction Account by this scheme (creditors' sacrifice plus cancellation) is:

options.explanation: This adds the full surrendered value to the sacrifice — but ₹2,40,000 of the surrendered shares was re-allotted, not sacrificed.
This counts only the cancellation — the creditors' ₹20,000 sacrifice is a reconstruction credit too.
Sacrifice ₹20,000 + cancellation ₹60,000 = ₹80,000 available to absorb write-offs.
This counts only the creditors' sacrifice and misses the ₹60,000 cancellation credit.

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Case: Bhima Alloys (reduction + account)
questions.case: Bhima Alloys Ltd's Tribunal-confirmed scheme provides: (1) the 1,20,000 equity shares of ₹10 fully paid are reduced to ₹5 fully paid (face value ₹5); (2) plant is revalued upwards by ₹45,000; (3) debenture holders owed ₹4,00,000 accept new debentures of ₹3,25,000 in full settlement; (4) the following are written off: P&L debit balance ₹5,10,000, goodwill ₹95,000, discount on issue of debentures ₹15,000; (5) scheme expenses of ₹10,000 are paid in cash and absorbed by the scheme; (6) any balance goes to capital reserve.

questions.stem: The credit to Capital Reduction from the equity capital write-down is:

options.explanation: That is the whole pre-scheme capital — half of it survives as ₹5-paid shares.
₹90,000 is the final capital-reserve balance, not the capital sacrifice.
₹7,20,000 is the account's total credit side — the capital element alone is ₹6,00,000.

questions.sourceRef: Companies Act 2013, s.66(1)(b)(ii); SM Ch 14 entries

questions.stem: Total credits to the Capital Reduction Account are:

options.explanation: This counts only the capital reduction and ignores both outside credits.
This omits the ₹45,000 plant appreciation.
This omits the debenture holders' ₹75,000 sacrifice.
Capital 6,00,000 + plant appreciation 45,000 + debenture holders' sacrifice (4,00,000 − 3,25,000) 75,000 = ₹7,20,000.

questions.stem: The transfer to Capital Reserve is:

options.explanation: This omits the ₹15,000 discount on issue of debentures — a fictitious asset the scheme writes off.

options.text: Nil
options.explanation: Credits exceed debits by ₹90,000 — the account cannot simply vanish; the surplus goes to capital reserve.
This forgets the ₹10,000 scheme expenses, which the account absorbs before anything reaches capital reserve.

questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 14 — balance of reconstruction account

questions.stem: Paid-up equity capital in the post-scheme balance sheet is:

options.explanation: This lumps the ₹90,000 capital reserve into share capital — they are separate lines under Schedule III.
That is the pre-scheme figure — the write-down halved it.
₹7,20,000 is the reconstruction account's credit total, not the surviving capital.

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Meaning
questions.stem: What is internal reconstruction? How does it differ from external reconstruction? (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Internal reconstruction: reorganisation of the company's capital within the SAME entity — revaluing assets, reassessing liabilities, writing off accumulated losses against capital sacrifices, reducing paid-up values and/or varying class rights
Typical trigger: continuous losses leaving the balance sheet with a P&L debit balance, fictitious assets and overvalued assets — capital no longer represented by available assets
External reconstruction: a NEW company is formed to take over the existing company's business, the old company is wound up; accounted as an amalgamation under AS 14 (s.232 machinery)
skeleton.citation: SM Ch 14 — types of reconstruction; AS 14

skeleton.point: Internal reconstruction runs on ss. 61/66 and the Capital Reduction Account; losses are absorbed internally, not left behind in a dying entity

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · s.61 alterations
questions.stem: State the ways in which a company may alter its share capital under section 61 of the Companies Act 2013, and the procedure required. (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Procedure: authorisation in the articles + ordinary resolution in general meeting; no Tribunal confirmation; notice of alteration to the Registrar
Increase of authorised share capital
Consolidation into larger denominations and sub-division into smaller denominations (paid/unpaid proportion preserved)
Conversion of fully paid shares into stock, and reconversion of that stock back into fully paid shares
Cancellation of shares not taken or agreed to be taken — diminishes authorised capital; expressly NOT a reduction of share capital

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Sub-division & consolidation
questions.stem: Explain the proportion rule applicable on sub-division or consolidation of shares, with an example and the accounting effect. (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Rule: the proportion between paid-up and unpaid amounts on each share must remain the same before and after the repackaging
Example: a ₹100 share with ₹75 paid sub-divided into ₹10 shares → ten shares each ₹7.50 paid, ₹2.50 uncalled
Accounting effect: old capital account closed into the new one; total paid-up capital unchanged; no Capital Reduction Account

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · s.66 reduction
questions.stem: Describe the procedure and the three modes of reduction of share capital under section 66 of the Companies Act 2013. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Procedure: special resolution + application to the Tribunal; NCLT confirms after considering creditors' representations; confirmed order registered with the Registrar
Mode 1: extinguish or reduce shareholders' liability for UNCALLED capital (e.g. ₹10 share ₹7.50 paid becomes ₹7.50 fully paid) — creates no Capital Reduction credit
Mode 2: pay off paid-up capital in excess of the company's needs — cash returns to shareholders; again no reconstruction credit
Mode 3: cancel paid-up capital that is lost or unrepresented by available assets — the sacrifice is credited to the Capital Reduction Account and absorbs the write-offs

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Capital Reduction Account
questions.stem: List the usual credits and debits to the Capital Reduction (Reconstruction) Account and state how its final balance is dealt with. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Credits: paid-up capital cancelled (equity and preference); reserves given up by shareholders; sacrifices by creditors and debenture holders under compromises; appreciation on revaluation of assets; unutilised surrendered shares cancelled
Debits: P&L debit balance; goodwill and worthless intangibles; fictitious assets (preliminary expenses, discount on issue); overvaluation written off assets and inventory; expenses of the scheme
Balance: a remaining credit is transferred to Capital Reserve; if write-offs exceed the credits, existing reserves in the books are applied to the shortfall
skeleton.citation: SM Ch 14 — balance of reconstruction account

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Compromise entries
questions.stem: Give the journal entries when (a) equity shareholders give up their claim over reserves, and (b) creditors agree to accept a lesser amount in settlement. (4 marks)

skeleton.point: (a) Reserves A/c Dr (amount of reserves given up) → To Reconstruction A/c
(b) Outside Liabilities (Creditors) A/c Dr (amount of sacrifice) and Provision A/c Dr (if any) → To Reconstruction A/c
Both sacrifices enlarge the pool available to write off losses; the surviving liability continues at the settled figure

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Surrender of shares
questions.stem: Explain the surrender-of-shares method of internal reconstruction and the treatment of surrendered shares. (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Shares are first sub-divided into smaller denominations, and shareholders surrender an agreed proportion to the company for use in the scheme

Surrendered shares are re-allotted to debenture holders and creditors in discharge of their claims, reducing outside liabilities; any shortfall accepted by them is their sacrifice, credited to the Reconstruction Account
Surrendered shares NOT re-allotted are cancelled, the paid-up value being credited to the Reconstruction Account

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Post-scheme presentation
questions.stem: State the special points to be kept in mind while preparing the balance sheet of a company after a scheme of internal reconstruction. (3 marks)

skeleton.point: "and Reduced" is added after the company's name ONLY if the Tribunal so orders
The amount written off fixed assets under the scheme must be disclosed for five years
New (reduced) share capital, capital reserve from the scheme surplus, and assets at written-down values presented per Schedule III; accumulated losses should no longer appear

questions.topic: Ch 14 · Internal Reconstruction · Conversion to stock
questions.stem: Write a short note on conversion of shares into stock. (3 marks)

skeleton.point: Under s.61(1)(c) a company may convert its fully paid shares into stock, and reconvert that stock into fully paid shares; only FULLY PAID shares qualify
Stock is capital in fused form, transferable in any amount rather than in unit shares; members' rights otherwise continue
skeleton.citation: SM Ch 14 — conversion of shares into stock

skeleton.point: Entry: Equity Share Capital A/c Dr → To Equity Stock A/c (and the reverse on reconversion); no Capital Reduction Account involved
